findings.location: AC: 'Layer-1 deterministic criteria implemented in Python, polyglot, fail-open: readiness shape (standardized `## Acceptance Criteria` across all types), file/symbol/import resolution (LSP/ast-grep), package existence (syft + registry + fallback), oversize signals, task-DAG validity, AC/DD quality, destructive sniff.'
findings.finding: Several Layer-1 criteria bundle multiple sub-checks (e.g., 'AC/DD quality' and 'destructive sniff') without defining pass/fail thresholds or what 'quality' means concretely. An executing agent cannot determine without clarification what specific conditions constitute a pass for 'AC/DD quality' or 'destructive sniff.' These are not inferrable from the plan text or standard conventions. Similarly, 'oversize signals' does not define the threshold (lines, tokens, story points?) an agent would use. These are defaultable gaps rather than blockers, since the agent can pick a reasonable interpretation, but they risk divergent implementations across child tickets.
findings.suggested_edit: For each Layer-1 check, add a one-line 'pass condition' in the AC or point to the DSO spec (docs/experiments/data/dso-criteria-specs.md) explicitly. E.g., 'oversize signals: ticket body > N tokens or > M files triggers a flag (see DSO spec §P5).'

findings.location: AC: 'Tests + verification: every AC maps to a concrete proving check; fail-open is tested per tool... plus tests for signature invalidation-on-material-edit, `--force` override logging, cap-hit INDETERMINATE shedding... and the reducer-ignored REVIEW_RESULT sidecar'
Context section: '~44% of agent failures are spec/plan defects'; 'rebar has a completion-verifier close gate... It has no equivalent at the start of work'

Children section lists 9 child tickets covering distinct concerns (registry, Layer-1, Layer-2, overlay routing, attestation, claim integration, chunking/budget, sidecar event, docs); Self-review note: 'G5 finding is CORRECT and intentionally left open: this epic should be decomposed — the explicit NEXT action is to split it into the one-wave implementation plan'

Section '## Experiment-grounded defaults (round 3; config-overridable)': 'Grounded by 5 experiment rounds (~1,600 LLM runs). Full design: docs/experiments/plan-review-criteria-storage-and-chunking.md.' Self-review note also references the gate being run on the epic itself.

AC: 'Tests + verification: every AC maps to a concrete proving check; fail-open is tested per tool (unsupported-lang / no-server / crash / timeout → skipped=pass), plus tests for signature invalidation-on-material-edit, --force override logging, cap-hit INDETERMINATE shedding (agent/overlay-first), and the reducer-ignored REVIEW_RESULT sidecar (reducer skips the type; head/.cache/deps/validate unaffected).'
findings.finding: The plan enumerates a solid set of test scenarios (fail-open per tool, signature invalidation, --force override, cap-hit shedding, reducer skip) and explicitly states 'every AC maps to a concrete proving check.' However, the plan does not name a RED-test-first dependency edge for any unit (TDD check (d)), does not specify how LLM/agentic calls are isolated in tests (e.g. mocking/stubbing the LLM client for Layer-2 criteria), and does not address boundary inputs for the chunking/budget logic (e.g. zero-criterion tickets, tickets with only DET criteria, malformed REVIEW_RESULT payloads). It is unclear whether these are intentionally left to child tickets (several children exist) or are genuinely omitted. Given child tickets are in scope, this may be adequately covered there — hence AMBIGUOUS rather than FAIL.
findings.suggested_edit: Add to the AC: (1) a note that LLM/tool calls in tests must be isolated via stubs/mocks (no live external calls in unit tests); (2) boundary cases for the chunker — zero-criteria, all-DET, malformed payload; (3) if TDD is the team norm, name at least one RED test per child ticket in the decomposition.

findings.location: Design summary: 'claim is a FAST signature-verify check only (no LLM, no network round-trip; target p95 < ~50ms)'; Budget section: 'small task ~$0.15–0.40; heavy epic ~$1.5–2.0 + minutes'; 'AGENT/tool-using … ~$0.12 + ~50s each'
findings.finding: The plan explicitly bounds the hot-path claim check (p95 < ~50ms, no LLM, no network), documents out-of-band execution for the heavy review, provides per-tier cost and latency estimates (single-turn ~$0.005–0.008, agent ~$0.12 + ~50s), states a per-plan budget cap with INDETERMINATE shedding on cap-hit, and addresses N+1 avoidance by chunking same-facet criteria and running DET criteria in parallel. Scalability is addressed via proportionate-depth routing and chunk-size scaling by ticket complexity. Performance characteristics are sound for the described scope.

findings.location: Design summary: 'fail-open on any unsupported language/tool (skipped = pass)'; 'Per-plan budget cap, chunk-atomic checkpointing; INDETERMINATE on cap-hit'; AC: 'fail-open is tested per tool (unsupported-lang / no-server / crash / timeout → skipped=pass)'
findings.finding: The plan addresses fail-open for external tool failures (LSP, ast-grep, syft, scc/lizard) and chunk-atomic checkpointing for budget-cap scenarios, and specifies INDETERMINATE as the outcome on cap-hit. However, there is no explicit statement of error handling or retry/backoff behavior for the LLM calls themselves (e.g. transient API errors, rate-limiting, partial responses). The plan is silent on whether a failed LLM chunk is retried, marked INDETERMINATE, or bubbles up as an error. Idempotency of the `REVIEW_RESULT` sidecar write (e.g. if the review is re-run mid-flight) is not addressed. These may be inherited from the existing `rebar.llm` extension points, but that assumption is not stated. Marking AMBIGUOUS because the existing rebar.llm runner may already handle this — codebase access would be needed to confirm.
findings.suggested_edit: Add an explicit statement to the reliability section: (1) LLM call failures (rate-limit, transient error) → treated as INDETERMINATE for that chunk, logged in REVIEW_RESULT; (2) REVIEW_RESULT sidecar writes are idempotent (re-running a review for the same material commit replaces or deduplicates the prior event).

findings.location: Design summary: 'HMAC under the MCP environment key — integrity/forgery/replay/staleness model inherited, no new key custody'; AC: 'Attestation: signing + material-event-commit binding + invalidation-on-material-edit + coverage record, reusing the close-gate signing machinery'
findings.finding: The plan introduces no new endpoints, no new data storage of PII, and no new auth boundaries — it reuses the existing MCP environment key and close-gate HMAC signing machinery. The attestation is a local signing artifact, not a network-exposed endpoint. The REVIEW_RESULT sidecar event contains per-criterion verdicts and finding fingerprints, but the plan does not store sensitive data and explicitly inherits the existing integrity/forgery/replay/staleness model. No OWASP Top 10 concerns are introduced by the described design.

findings.location: Design summary: 'Polyglot tooling (fail-open IRONCLAD): Layer-1 wraps external tools, ALL added as NEW integrations by this epic (none are wired into rebar today): LSP resolution via solidlsp … ast-grep … syft … scc + lizard'; 'reuse rebar.llm's catalog/contracts/prompt/runner'; AC: 'Criteria registry (declarative descriptors) + generic orchestrator, reusing rebar.llm extension points.'
findings.finding: The plan introduces several new cross-component integrations (solidlsp/Serena MCP, ast-grep, syft, scc, lizard) and a new criteria registry + orchestrator that hooks into rebar.llm. The plan states these are 'fail-open wrappers' and acknowledges they are new integrations, which partially addresses coupling_risk. Config-overridability for chunking, budget caps, and per-criterion blocking addresses changeability. However, there is no explicit mention of an ADR or design doc update for the novel architectural decisions introduced here (criteria registry pattern, the REVIEW_RESULT reducer-ignored sidecar event type, the two-layer verdict model). The plan references 'Full design detail lives in session log 63cc-ec94-4792-464b' but does not commit to capturing these decisions in a durable, discoverable artifact (ADR/CLAUDE.md/design doc). This is AMBIGUOUS because the docs child ticket (a473-8af4-a493-4e0e) may cover this — but the plan text does not make that explicit.
findings.suggested_edit: Add to the Docs child ticket scope (a473-8af4-a493-4e0e) or to a new child: an ADR or CLAUDE.md section covering (1) the criteria registry pattern and generic orchestrator design decision, (2) the REVIEW_RESULT reducer-ignored sidecar event type rationale, and (3) the two-layer verdict model. Reference these from the epic's design summary so the coupling_risk and documentation sub-checks are clearly satisfied.

findings.location: Acceptance Criteria, all three bullets
Scope — 'pack same-facet single-turn criteria up to the configured chunk size'
findings.finding: The chunk-size policy is explicitly deferred to a sibling story ('Out of scope: the chunk-size policy'), yet the generic chunker must accept and act on a chunk-size parameter. An executing agent would have to choose an arbitrary default value/unit (token count? criterion count? bytes?) to write and test the chunker. This is a defaultable gap, not a blocker, but it leaves the agent guessing at the unit of measurement. Similarly, 'deterministically' for ordering within a facet-bucket is asserted but no tie-breaking rule (e.g., alphabetical by id, declaration order) is specified.
findings.suggested_edit: Add one sentence: 'For the purposes of this story, chunk size is expressed as a maximum number of criteria per chunk (e.g., default=5); tie-breaking within a facet bucket is by alphabetical criterion id.' This lets the agent proceed without waiting on the sibling story.

findings.location: AC 2 — 'unit-tested on a fixture registry'; AC 3 — 'covered by a test that adds a fixture criterion and asserts it is packed correctly'
findings.finding: Both testable ACs point to unit tests as the proving command, which is good. However, AC 1 ('schema-validated criterion descriptor … stored in the rebar.llm catalog') has no described proving command — no mention of a schema-validation test, a CI lint step, or a validator invocation. An agent could satisfy the descriptor's presence but have no automated check that validates its structure. The plan describes the schema fields but does not specify what validates them (e.g., JSON Schema + ajv, Zod, Pydantic, CI step).
findings.suggested_edit: Add to AC 1: 'A schema validation test (e.g., ajv / Zod / Pydantic) runs in CI and rejects a malformed descriptor, proving the schema is enforced.'

findings.location: Why section — 'Every other workstream consumes criteria as data'
Title vs. What/Scope sections
Scope section and Acceptance Criteria
Scope — 'pack same-facet single-turn criteria up to the configured chunk size'
findings.finding: The facet-packing heuristic (grouping same-facet criteria into chunks) is a novel design choice whose effectiveness rests on an unvalidated assumption: that co-locating same-facet criteria into one LLM call produces better or equivalent results vs. other grouping strategies. The plan asserts this packing strategy without a spike or measurement to confirm it doesn't degrade LLM recall or cause context conflicts when facets are heterogeneous. The fixture unit tests validate mechanical correctness of the packer, but not whether the packing strategy is empirically sound. That said, this is a 'tunable default/threshold' situation — the strategy is adjustable — so severity is minor rather than major.
findings.suggested_edit: Consider adding a note: 'The facet-packing strategy is an initial heuristic; a follow-on empirical step (e.g., comparing review quality with N=1 vs. N=5 same-facet criteria per chunk) is planned in the chunking-routing-budget story.'

findings.location: Acceptance Criteria AC1 vs. Scope / What sections
findings.finding: AC1 ('A schema-validated criterion descriptor with the fields above, stored in the rebar.llm catalog') maps to the Scope field list and the 'What' section, so coverage is present. AC2 ('A generic chunker that … produces tier-bucketed, facet-packed chunks deterministically') maps to the 'What'/'Scope' chunker description. AC3 ('Adding or retuning a criterion requires only editing the registry') maps to the stated design goal. No orphan criteria found. However, there is a subtle terminology drift: the Scope section uses 'filter applicable criteria → bucket by exec tier → pack same-facet single-turn criteria up to the configured chunk size', but AC2 refers to 'a chunk size' (singular, as a parameter) while the 'Out of scope' note says 'chunk-size policy' is deferred to another story — it is not fully clear whether 'configured chunk size' in AC2 is a runtime parameter or a hard-coded placeholder value. Additionally, the Scope introduces the term 'prompt-runner extension points' and 'rebar.llm's catalog.json + contracts', but these terms are not referenced or defined in the Acceptance Criteria, leaving it ambiguous whether conformance to those contracts is actually verified anywhere.
findings.suggested_edit: In AC2, clarify whether 'chunk size' is injected as a test fixture constant or must honour an interface defined by the chunking-routing-budget story. Also add a note in AC1 that the descriptor schema must satisfy the existing rebar.llm catalog.json contract (or cite where that contract is checked).

findings.location: Acceptance Criteria AC2 and AC3
findings.finding: The testing plan is thin and addresses only the happy path. AC2 states the chunker is 'unit-tested on a fixture registry' and AC3 states 'a test that adds a fixture criterion and asserts it is packed correctly' — both describe only the normal, well-formed input scenario. The following failure/boundary paths are entirely absent: (1) empty descriptor set (what does the chunker emit?); (2) a descriptor with an unknown/invalid 'exec' or 'facet' value that fails schema validation; (3) an oversized chunk where no single criterion fits within the configured chunk size; (4) duplicate criterion IDs in the registry; (5) a descriptor missing required fields (e.g., no 'routing' or 'trigger'). Additionally, none of the named tests identify a specific RED test that fails before implementation (TDD dependency edges are missing), and 'packed correctly' is not an observable/measurable outcome — it needs a concrete assertion definition (e.g., exact output structure, ordering, grouping).
findings.suggested_edit: Add explicit unit test cases for: (a) empty input → empty output; (b) schema-invalid descriptor → validation error raised; (c) chunk size smaller than the smallest criterion → each criterion gets its own chunk; (d) duplicate IDs → deterministic deduplication or error. Replace 'packed correctly' with a concrete assertion such as 'output list equals expected fixture list with specific tier order and facet groupings'.

findings.location: Scope: 'pack same-facet single-turn criteria up to the configured chunk size → emit one unit per AGENT criterion'
findings.finding: The plan introduces a chunker that processes a descriptor set and emits chunks — this is a batch compute operation whose output feeds downstream LLM calls (implied by the 'prompt-runner extension points' and 'AGENT' tier). The plan states no latency target, no input-size limit, and no statement about the algorithmic complexity of facet-packing. Concretely: packing same-facet criteria naively could be O(n²) if implemented as repeated linear scans. More critically, 'emit one unit per AGENT criterion' means the number of LLM call units grows linearly with the number of AGENT-typed criteria — an O(n) LLM-calls-per-item pattern — but no cap or concurrency limit is stated. No done-definition around acceptable chunking time or maximum registry size is present.
findings.suggested_edit: Add a scalability note: state the expected maximum registry size (e.g., ≤200 criteria), assert O(n log n) or better packing complexity, and specify whether AGENT units are dispatched concurrently with a pool/rate-limit cap or sequentially.

findings.location: Scope / What section
findings.finding: The plan adds no external integrations, file writes, or stateful transitions — it reads a registry (catalog.json) and produces chunk structures in memory. No write operations, no LLM calls within the chunker itself, and no mutable shared state are introduced. Reliability concerns are not applicable at this layer.

findings.location: Plan overall
findings.finding: The plan introduces no new endpoints, data storage, PII handling, or auth boundaries. It reads a local catalog.json and produces in-memory data structures. Security overlay is not applicable.

findings.location: What: 'Reuse rebar.llm's catalog.json + contracts + prompt-runner extension points'; Scope: descriptor field list; 'Out of scope: chunk-size policy (chunking-routing-budget story)'
findings.finding: The plan introduces a new architectural pattern — a declarative criterion descriptor stored in rebar.llm's catalog — and a generic orchestrator/chunker that reads it. This crosses at least two component boundaries (the criteria registry and the downstream prompt-runner). Three maintainability sub-checks fail: (a) Coupling risk: the dependency on 'rebar.llm's catalog.json + contracts + prompt-runner extension points' is named but not justified or mitigated with an interface boundary. It is unclear whether the chunker calls into the prompt-runner directly or emits a data structure consumed by it. (b) Changeability: the descriptor fields (especially 'exec' tier values DET | 1-TURN | 2-STEP | AGENT and 'block_policy' values advisory | blocking-with-override) are enumerated inline in the Scope section with no mention of where these enumerations live or how they can be extended without a code change. (c) Documentation: the plan introduces a novel architectural decision (declarative registry as the single source of truth for criteria routing) but references no ADR, CLAUDE.md update, or design doc.
findings.suggested_edit: Add an ADR or CLAUDE.md section documenting the registry-as-source-of-truth decision. Explicitly define whether the chunker emits a typed data contract consumed by the prompt-runner (and name that contract). Store the 'exec' and 'block_policy' enumerations in the schema itself, not just in prose, so they are machine-checkable and extensible without touching orchestrator code.

findings.location: AC bullet 2: 'proven by a fixture test per failure mode (in src/rebar/...)'
findings.finding: Most ACs are observable and in-session completable (fixture tests, PASS/FAIL/skipped output, coverage record). However, AC-2 says 'proven by a fixture test per failure mode' but does not specify which failure modes must be covered (unsupported-lang, no-server, crash, timeout are listed in the Scope but not formally enumerated in the AC). An agent could ship fixtures for a subset and consider it done. AC-3 uses the phrase 'weak/absent tooling yields could-not-confirm (skipped)' — 'weak' is not a precisely defined trigger condition, leaving the boundary between skipped and FAIL slightly subjective.
findings.suggested_edit: In AC-2, enumerate the four required failure-mode fixture tests explicitly: (1) unsupported language, (2) server unreachable, (3) tool crash/exception, (4) timeout. This makes the criterion fully binary-checkable.

findings.location: Scope bullet: 'solidlsp (reachable via the configured Serena MCP server; to be wired, not a rebar library)'; Tool wrappers section; AC-3 'hard missing verdicts come only from a top-tier server'
findings.finding: Multiple blocking ambiguities exist: (1) 'top-tier server' in AC-3 is undefined — no server tier taxonomy is provided, so an agent cannot determine the threshold between a server that may emit FAIL vs one that must yield skipped. (2) The Serena MCP server is described as 'configured' and 'reachable' with no details about how it is configured, where its config lives, what protocol/auth is expected, or how to verify reachability in CI — an agent would need to stop and ask. (3) 'per-ecosystem registry + LLM-fallback' for P3 package existence is mentioned without specifying which registries, how LLM-fallback is invoked/configured, or what the fallback response schema looks like. (4) The relationship between ast-grep and solidlsp for P2 is unstated — when does one take precedence over the other?
findings.suggested_edit: Define 'top-tier server' with an explicit list or property (e.g., 'a server that returns a non-error response within N seconds'). Add a configuration reference for the Serena MCP server (env-var name or config file path). Enumerate the target registries for P3. Specify the fallback invocation interface.

findings.location: AC bullet 1 (P1-P7 implemented); AC bullet 4 (coverage recorded); Scope section (P1–P7 list)
findings.finding: No proving command or end-to-end verification step is described for any of the seven checks (P1–P7). AC-1 says each check 'emits PASS/FAIL/skipped with a cited reason' but provides no command to invoke them together or validate their outputs. AC-4 says coverage is 'recorded for the attestation' without specifying where it is written, how it is read back, or what command proves it is present. AC-3 (hard-missing only from top-tier server) has no mapped implementation step. The plan's steps section is effectively absent — there is a scope list but no procedural steps, so the union-of-steps-to-end-state test fails: there are no steps to union.
findings.suggested_edit: Add an explicit verification section: e.g., 'Run `pytest src/rebar/tests/ -v` to confirm fixture tests for all failure modes pass; run `python -m rebar check --fixture=sample_pr` and assert each of P1–P7 appears in output with a verdict and reason; inspect the attestation output file for a coverage block listing ran/skipped per check.'

findings.location: Why section: 'the deterministic floor is the only tier that BLOCKS, so it must be sound (near-zero FP) and must generalize across the diverse client base via fail-open tooling'
Title vs Scope/AC sections
findings.finding: The title promises 'Layer-1 deterministic floor (P1-P7) + fail-open tool wrappers'. The body delivers exactly: P1–P7 checks with PASS/FAIL/skipped output, four named tool wrappers (solidlsp, ast-grep, syft, scc/lizard), and an explicit fail-open IRONCLAD rule. No material scope drift detected.

findings.location: Scope section listing P1–P7 plus four tool wrappers; AC bullets 1–4
findings.finding: This ticket spans seven distinct checks (P1–P7), four external tool integrations (solidlsp, ast-grep, syft, scc+lizard), a new MCP server wiring, a coverage-recording mechanism, and a fixture test suite — touching many files across multiple layers (check logic, tool wrappers, tests, config, attestation output). It fails the single-concern test: it is structurally an 'AND' joining independent deliverables (e.g., P3 package-existence is entirely independent of P5 task-DAG validity; syft integration is independent of ast-grep integration). Any one of the tool wrappers or any two of the P-checks would be a coherent leaf ticket. As a monolithic ticket, the risk of partial delivery or an agent losing coherence is high.
findings.suggested_edit: Decompose into at minimum: (a) a ticket for the fail-open tool-wrapper framework + fixture harness; (b) one ticket per tool integration (solidlsp/ast-grep, syft, scc/lizard); (c) one ticket per P-check group (e.g., P1–P2 resolution, P3 package, P4 oversize, P5 DAG, P6 AC/DD, P7 destructive sniff). Each child ticket references the shared framework.

findings.location: Scope: 'solidlsp (reachable via the configured Serena MCP server; to be wired, not a rebar library)'; Scope: 'syft + per-ecosystem registry + LLM-fallback'; Scope: 'scc + lizard'; Fail-open IRONCLAD rule
findings.finding: Several novel/unverified assumptions exist with no validation plan: (1) solidlsp via the Serena MCP server is described as a new integration ('none currently in repo') — it is unverified whether the MCP server is reachable in CI, what its latency is, and whether it produces symbol-resolution results of sufficient quality to support near-zero-FP verdicts. (2) The LLM-fallback for P3 package existence is asserted as a viable path but its error rate, latency, and cost are entirely unmeasured — a hallucinating LLM could produce false positives/negatives at significant rates. (3) The claim 'near-zero FP' for the combined P1–P7 floor is asserted without any measurement plan, benchmark, or sample corpus. No spike, prototype, or pilot with success metrics is described for any of these novel integrations.
findings.suggested_edit: Before full build-out, add a spike ticket that: (a) verifies the Serena MCP / solidlsp connection is reachable from the agent environment with a latency measurement; (b) samples 20–30 real PRs through a candidate P2 check and measures FP/FN rates; (c) tests the LLM-fallback against a known set of packages and records accuracy. Gate the main build on spike results.

findings.location: Acceptance Criteria, all four bullets
findings.finding: The ACs describe structural properties of the output (e.g., 'registry descriptor with exec/facet + binary checklist + anti-FP rule', 'validated against the REVIEW_RESULT schema', 'covered by a positive and a negative fixture test'), which are observable repo artifacts. However, 'validated against the REVIEW_RESULT schema' assumes the schema exists and is reachable; without knowing whether REVIEW_RESULT is already defined in the repo, it is unclear whether this check is in-session completable or depends on prior work. Similarly, 'binary checklist + anti-FP rule' is observable in code but 'binary' is not defined (boolean flags? a two-item list?). These are thin ambiguities, not blockers, but they slightly reduce measurability.
findings.suggested_edit: Explicitly reference the path/module where REVIEW_RESULT is defined (e.g., 'validated against the schema in src/schemas/review_result.py') and define what 'binary checklist' means structurally (e.g., 'a list of yes/no check strings').

findings.location: Scope section — 'Checklists sourced from docs/experiments/data/dso-criteria-specs.md'
findings.finding: The plan references a source document ('dso-criteria-specs.md') but does not specify which sections, tables, or item-identifiers within that document map to each criterion. An executing agent must read the whole document and decide independently which content to use, which is a real design choice deferred to the executor. Additionally, 'registry descriptor' is undefined — the plan never describes the schema, fields, or file location of the registry, so the agent cannot know what shape to produce. The Scope bullet 'EXP empirical-validation-for-complex-plans (generic; suppressed if the plan already includes experimentation)' uses the word 'generic' without specifying what 'generic' means structurally vs. the other criteria. These gaps collectively could cause the agent to stop and ask.
findings.suggested_edit: Add a pointer to the specific sections/item IDs in dso-criteria-specs.md for each criterion, define the registry descriptor schema (fields: id, exec, facet, checklist[], anti_fp_rule, prompt_template) with a reference to its file location, and replace 'generic' with a concrete description of how EXP differs from the other descriptors.

findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC3 ('EXP criterion fires only when… covered by a positive and a negative fixture test') and AC4 ('Container criteria (G3/G4) run only when the ticket has_children')
findings.finding: AC3 mentions 'a positive and a negative fixture test' as the proving mechanism, which is concrete. However, AC4 ('Container criteria run only when has_children') has no stated proving command — there is no fixture test, CLI invocation, or CI check described that would demonstrate the conditional gate is actually enforced. AC2 states findings are 'validated against the REVIEW_RESULT schema' but no command or test is named to run this validation. The plan also has no step describing how the agent will verify that every criterion's checklist and anti-FP rule are correctly implemented end-to-end (e.g., an integration test suite or a known-bad plan that should trigger each criterion).
findings.suggested_edit: For AC4, add a fixture test (e.g., 'a childless ticket plan must not trigger G3/G4; a ticket with has_children=true must trigger them — covered by fixture tests G3_no_children_suppressed and G3_with_children_fires'). For AC2, name the validation command (e.g., 'pytest tests/test_schema_validation.py').

findings.location: Why section — 'The advisory criteria are where the gate earns its keep — coaching agents to better plans.'
Title vs. What/Scope sections
Scope section — lists 11 distinct criteria (F1, F4, E2, E5, G5, E1, E3, E6, E4, G1+G2, A1, G3, G4, EXP)
findings.finding: The ticket bundles 11 distinct advisory criteria across three execution-mode families (single-turn, 2-step, agentic) plus the EXP and container criteria. Each criterion requires its own registry descriptor, prompt/checklist, and anti-FP rule. This is a high-breadth ticket that touches many schema entries and likely multiple files. Without knowing the repo structure it is unclear whether all 11 can be coherently executed in one session, or whether the work is structured as a single coherent module addition (which would make it acceptable). If each criterion maps to a separate file/module, this strongly signals decomposition into at least 3 child tickets (by family) would reduce risk.
findings.suggested_edit: Consider decomposing into three child tickets aligned with the three families (single-turn, 2-step, agentic+container) so each can be reviewed, tested, and merged independently. If the registry is a single append-only file, note that explicitly so sizing is clear.

findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC3 ('covered by a positive and a negative fixture test')
Acceptance Criteria, bullet 1: 'Each criterion above is a registry descriptor with exec/facet + a binary checklist + anti-FP rule.'
findings.finding: The plan body describes deliverables across three groups (single-turn, 2-step, agentic) and an EXP criterion, and AC-1 covers all of them under a single bullet. However, AC-1 does not call out the EXP criterion explicitly — it says 'each criterion above' which could be read to include EXP, but EXP's unique suppression logic is only addressed in AC-3. This creates a minor ambiguity: is EXP's registry-descriptor shape validated by AC-1 or only by AC-3? Additionally, the plan body lists 'G3 child-coverage + G4 child-consistency' as agentic criteria, but AC-4 calls them 'Container criteria (G3/G4)' — a category rename that is harmless but slightly inconsistent. No orphan or uncovered work was found; all described deliverables map to at least one AC. No duplicate requirements detected.
findings.suggested_edit: In AC-1, expand 'each criterion above' to explicitly list all groups: 'Each criterion (F1, F4, E2, E5, G5, E1, E3, E6, E4, G1, G2, A1, G3, G4, EXP) is a registry descriptor…' to eliminate ambiguity about EXP's inclusion. Align the label 'agentic container criteria' consistently across the plan body and AC-4.

findings.location: Acceptance Criteria bullets 3 and 4: 'The EXP criterion fires only when… covered by a positive and a negative fixture test.' and 'Container criteria (G3/G4) run only when the ticket has_children.'
findings.finding: The testing plan is fragmentary. AC-3 mandates a positive and a negative fixture test for EXP suppression, which is good, but: (a) No fixture tests or unit tests are specified for any of the 13 other criteria (F1, F4, E2, E5, G5, E1, E3, E6, E4, G1, G2, A1, G3, G4). For each of these, the plan adds new business logic (checklist evaluation, anti-FP rules, registry dispatch) with no stated RED tests or failure paths. (b) AC-4 states G3/G4 'run only when the ticket has_children' but provides no test verifying the suppression path (i.e., a ticket WITHOUT children must not trigger G3/G4 — the plan has no fixture for this). (c) No failure/invalid-input paths are addressed: what happens when a registry descriptor is malformed, when a plan provides no location, or when the REVIEW_RESULT schema validation fails? (d) The AC references 'validated against the REVIEW_RESULT schema' (AC-2) but names no test or validator that proves this. Outcomes are not observable/measurable — 'each finding follows the coaching spec' is not a testable assertion without a defined pass/fail harness.
findings.suggested_edit: Add an explicit testing section covering: (1) At least one positive + one negative fixture per criterion group (single-turn, 2-step, agentic) exercising the binary checklist and anti-FP rule; (2) A suppression fixture for G3/G4 with has_children=false; (3) A schema-validation test asserting REVIEW_RESULT compliance for at least one well-formed and one malformed finding; (4) A failure-path fixture for a malformed registry descriptor. Mark each with a declared RED→GREEN dependency edge.

Scope section: 'Checklists sourced from docs/experiments/data/dso-criteria-specs.md. Each finding follows the coaching spec.' and AC-1: 'Each criterion above is a registry descriptor with exec/facet + a binary checklist + anti-FP rule.'
findings.finding: The plan introduces a new registry-based pipeline stage (criterion descriptors with exec/facet dispatch) and a new contract shape (REVIEW_RESULT schema), both of which cross component boundaries (registry → prompt engine → schema validator → coaching output). However: (a) No ADR, CLAUDE.md entry, or design doc update is mentioned for the registry descriptor format or the dispatch mechanism — this is a novel architectural pattern with no documentation artifact called out. (b) The checklist source ('dso-criteria-specs.md') is referenced but no ownership, update, or versioning policy is stated — criteria thresholds/rules embedded in checklists are expected to evolve (the entire ticket is about adding judgment criteria) yet are described as static content with no changeability mechanism. (c) The coupling between the registry descriptor shape and the REVIEW_RESULT schema is load-bearing but undocumented; a change to either would silently break the other with no stated interface boundary or contract test.

(b) Note that dso-criteria-specs.md is the authoritative source and describe a process for keeping registry checklists in sync when the spec evolves.
